coredata.dc:title: Protective effect of carbon dots as antioxidants on intestinal inflammation by regulating oxidative stress and gut microbiota in nematodes and mouse models
coredata.dc:creator: Peng, Nannan
Wang, Jingmin
Zhu, Haimei
Liu, Ziyue
Ren, Jiayi
Li, Wenjing
Wang, Yongzhong
coredata.dc:description: Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) is a recurrent chronic colitis disease with increasing incidence and prevalence year by year. The single efficacy and significant side effects of traditional IBD treatment drugs have promoted the flourishing development of new drugs. Inspired by many health benefits of carbon dots (CDs) based nanomedicine in biomedical applications, a metal-free carbon dots (CP-CDs) was synthesized from citric acid and polyethylene polyamine to treat colitis. Oxidative stress tests at the cellular and nematode levels demonstrated CP-CDs have good antioxidant effects, while the toxicity of CP-CDs to cells and nematodes is low. CP-CDs were further applied to dextran sodium sulfate (DSS)-induced colitis in mice models, and it was found that CP-CDs can reduce the disease activity index (DAI) score of colon tissue and restore the intestinal barrier. Further, the anti-colitis mechanisms of CP-CDs were explored, one of which is to regulate intestinal oxidative stress in inflammatory mice, further reducing the expression of inflammatory cytokines, and thus alleviating colitis. Notably, 16S rRNA sequence analysis showed that the abundance of beneficial bacteria (Ligilactobacillus and Enterorhabdus) in the intestinal tract increased, while that of harmful bacteria (unclassified_Clostridia_UCG_014) decreased after CP-CDs treatment, indicating that CP-CDs rebalancing the gut microbiota destroyed by DSS is another important mechanism. In short, these non-toxic carbon dots not only have the potential for multi-factor combined relief of colitis but also offer an alternative therapy medicine for patients suffering from IBD.

In vitro anti-oxidative and anti-inflammatory effects of the CP-CDs. (f) SOD, (g) GSH, (h) T-AOC and (i) MDA content in different treatment groups under the oxidative stress model. mRNA levels of oxidative stress-related genes (j) SOD-5, (k) GST-10, and (l) CTL-1 and (m) nematode longevity-related genes Skn-1 in different treatment groups. Mean ± SEM. n = 3, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01.

(g) Relative expression of Occludin, (h) Claudin-1, (i) ZO-1, and (j) MUC-2 mRNA in colon tissue of mice in each group. Mean ± SEM. n = 3, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001.

CP-CDs regulate oxidative stress and down-regulates NLRP3 pathway inflammatory cytokines in colitis mice. Redox-related indicators of (a) GSH, (b) SOD and (c) MDA contents in the colon tissues from healthy and colitis mice. Mean ± SEM. n = 3, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001. Serum contents of pro-inflammatory factor (d) TNF-α and (e) IL-1β, (f) anti-inflammatory factor IL-10 in mice of different treatment groups. Mean ± SEM. n = 3, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001. (g–j) Gene expression: Relative transcript level of pro-inflammatory (NLRP3, ASC, Caspase-1, IL-1β) cytokines in colon tissues, as determined by RT-qPCR. Mean ± SEM. n = 3, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001. (k) NLRP3 signaling pathway mechanism diagram.

CP-CDs restore the changes in intestinal microbiota composition. (a) The Venn diagram in mice of different treatment groups. (b) Assessment of β diversity based on principal coordinate analysis (PCoA). (c) The average relative abundance of taxa at the phylum and (d) genus level. (e–g) Relative abundance of discriminative gut microbial community members at the genus level. (e) Ligilactobacillus. (f) Enterorhabdus. (g) unclassified_Clostridia_UCG_014. Mean ± SEM. n = 6, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01.

head.ce:title: Protective effect of carbon dots as antioxidants on intestinal inflammation by regulating oxidative stress and gut microbiota in nematodes and mouse models

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) is a recurrent chronic colitis disease with increasing incidence and prevalence year by year. The single efficacy and significant side effects of traditional IBD treatment drugs have promoted the flourishing development of new drugs. Inspired by many health benefits of carbon dots (CDs) based nanomedicine in biomedical applications, a metal-free carbon dots (CP-CDs) was synthesized from citric acid and polyethylene polyamine to treat colitis. Oxidative stress tests at the cellular and nematode levels demonstrated CP-CDs have good antioxidant effects, while the toxicity of CP-CDs to cells and nematodes is low. CP-CDs were further applied to dextran sodium sulfate (DSS)-induced colitis in mice models, and it was found that CP-CDs can reduce the disease activity index (DAI) score of colon tissue and restore the intestinal barrier. Further, the anti-colitis mechanisms of CP-CDs were explored, one of which is to regulate intestinal oxidative stress in inflammatory mice, further reducing the expression of inflammatory cytokines, and thus alleviating colitis. Notably, 16S rRNA sequence analysis showed that the abundance of beneficial bacteria (Ligilactobacillus and Enterorhabdus) in the intestinal tract increased, while that of harmful bacteria (unclassified_Clostridia_UCG_014) decreased after CP-CDs treatment, indicating that CP-CDs rebalancing the gut microbiota destroyed by DSS is another important mechanism. In short, these non-toxic carbon dots not only have the potential for multi-factor combined relief of colitis but also offer an alternative therapy medicine for patients suffering from IBD.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), including ulcerative colitis (UC) and Crohn's disease (CD), is a type of chronic digestive tract disease characterized by dysregulation of the intestinal immune system [1]. IBD patients may suffer from diarrhea, abdominal pain, anal lesions, and even complications (such as intra-abdominal abscess, intestinal obstruction, and acute intestinal perforation, etc.) [2], which not only seriously affect patients' quality of life, but also bring economic and medical burdens to society [3]. Clinically prescribed medications have been used to relieve symptoms, including 5-aminosalicylic acid (5-ASA) [4], corticosteroids [5], and immunosuppressants [6]. Nevertheless, the therapeutic limitations of these drugs are regrettable, such as irreversible consumption, rapid metabolism, and non-selective interactions [7]. Therefore, it is urgent to develop alternative drugs to expand treatment options for IBD patients.

Currently, an increasing number of nanomedicines are being applied to treat IBD, which mainly act as antioxidants in inflammation to reduce high levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the colon and alleviate damage caused by oxidative stress. For instance, Sun et al. [8] reported a strategy for the synthesis of halogens-doped single-atom cobalt catalysts anchored on nitrogen-doped carbon (CoCNI) through self-assembly and pyrolysis processes. The experiment proved that CoCNI has a significant ROS elimination efficiency, which can protect cells from oxidative stress, thus demonstrating excellent anti-colitis effects. Co-NSs was reported to be a 2D structured nanoenzyme with high catalytic activity, which can not only exert ROS clearance activity but also synergistically inhibit the activation of NLPR3 inflammasome, thereby reducing the progression of inflammation-related diseases [9]. Besides, polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) coated NiCo2O4 nanoparticles [10], Mo3Se4 nano flakes [11], etc. also have good therapeutic effects on colitis, confirming that those nano-antioxidants are promising therapeutic agents for treating IBD. However, these studies overlooked an important factor in exploring the mechanism of nanomedicine therapy for IBD – the gut microbiota. During IBD, the breakdown of the intestinal barrier increases the invasion and transport of bacteria, thus affecting the gut microbiota. The imbalance of the gut microbiota can lead to an unbalanced immune response and aggravate intestinal inflammation [12–14]. Therefore, exploring the regulatory effect of nanomedicines on gut microbiota is an important step towards further realizing their application value. Nanocrystalline cellulose (NCC) synthesized from plant fibers can not only regulate the MAPK pathway of mucin 2 but also regulate the abundance of Akkermansia and Odoribacters to effectively treat UC [15]. Zhang et al. [16] reported that Bi2Se3 nanodiscs can restore intestinal homeostasis by increasing the ratio of Firmicutes to Bacteroidetes and inhibiting Proteobacteria to achieve therapeutic effects on IBD. And the Bi2Se3 also has excellent biocompatibility and nitrogen species (RONS) scavenging ability. Moreover, cerium oxide nanoenzymes and natural curcumin integrated nanoantioxidants (Cur-Ce-NPs) [17], and gold nanoclusters (Au29SG27) [18] have also been found to alleviate colitis by regulating gut microbiota. However, the complex synthesis of NCC and Cur-Ce-NPs, the in vivo biosafety of Bi2Se3 and the high cost of raw materials for Au29SG27 limit the application of these nanomedicines. Thus, the synthesis of low-cost, good biosafety nano-antioxidants and the study of their mechanisms for alleviating oxygen rebalance and regulating gut microbiota are of great significance for the search for alternative IBD therapeutic drugs.

Carbon dots (CDs), are a type of “core-shell” carbon nanoparticle with a size smaller than 10 nm, including carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs). They have the advantages of a wide range of raw materials, simple synthesis, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and easy surface functionalization, making them widely used in the biomedical field [19–21]. In recent years, the exploration of CDs as a novel nanomedicine for treating IBD has achieved many excellent results. The GQDs prepared by Byung-Chul Lee et al. [22] exhibited antioxidant effects, which can restore intestinal immune homeostasis by clearing reactive oxygen species (ROS), inhibiting excessive inflammation, and promoting the development of T cell regulatory loops. Mou et al. [13] synthesized carbon dots (SCR-CDs) from scrambled Coptidis Rhizoma, which not only enhanced the expression of intestinal TJ protein (tight junction, TJ), reduced inflammatory cell infiltration, and improved oxidative stress, but also increased the diversity of intestinal microbiota, restored the balance between beneficial bacteria and pathogenic bacteria, and thus alleviating colitis. The CDs from broccoli water extract (BWE-CDs) showed excellent free radical scavenging activity on DPPH (2, 2-diphenyl-1-picrylhy-drazyl) and ABTS (2, 2′-Azinobis- (3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulphonate)). The mechanism study showed that the anti-inflammatory effect of BWE-CDs depends on the direct reaction between CDs and free radical, the regulation of NO level, and the up-regulation of superoxide dismutase (SOD) and glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX-4) expression [23]. These research ideas on the green synthesis of CDs using natural products are worth learning from, but perhaps due to the complex composition of natural products (including pigments, proteins, cellulose, etc.), the stability of sources and batches, researchers are also attempting to synthesize CDs using organic/inorganic molecules and exploring their anti-IBD effects [24–26]. Se-CQDs developed by hydrothermal treatment of selenocysteine can effectively scavenge •OH, and Se-CQDs can effectively protect cells from oxidative stress by scavenging excess ROS in the cells. However, the antioxidant properties of Se-CQDs have only been validated at the cellular level [27]. Ma et al. [28] synthesized multifunctional carbon-dots nanomaterials from small molecules to protect cells from oxidative damage by clearing hydroxyl radicals, thus remodeling the redox environment of the colon but ignoring the role of gut microbiota. Therefore, it is a promising work to study the therapeutic mechanism of small molecule synthesized carbon dots with antioxidant properties for IBD.

In this study, novel metal-free CP-CDs with good free radical scavenging activity were synthesized using citric acid and polyethylene polyamine as precursors and applied to relieve DSS-induced colitis (Fig. 1 ). In vitro cell experiments showed that CP-CDs not only have low toxicity but also exhibit good potential in antioxidant and anti-inflammatory applications. Caenorhabditis elegans (C. elegans) stimulated by juglone was used as a model in vivo. Results from ROS mean fluorescence quantization, oxidative stress, and RT-qPCR indicated that CP-CDs were well-suited for antioxidant applications. In addition, the body length and locomotion capacity of C. elegans also indicated the biosafety of CP-CDs. Meanwhile, the anti-inflammatory application value of CP-CDs in DSS-induced IBD model mice was validated, including weight gain, increased colon length, and intestinal barrier recovery. Further investigation on the mechanism proved that CP-CDs can reduce oxidative stress in colon tissue and significantly down-regulate inflammatory cytokines in the NLRP3 inflammatory pathway, thereby alleviating colitis. More importantly, 16S rRNA sequence analysis showed that CP-CDs re-balanced the intestinal microflora by inhibiting Proteobacteria and increasing unclassified_Muribaculacea, Ligilactobacillus. Thus, these non-metallic CP-CDs achieve anti-inflammatory effects through a multi-pronged intervention strategy, providing not only a new nanomedicine for IBD but also new ideas for the therapeutic mechanism of nanomedicine.

Citric acid and polyethylene polyamine were purchased from Shanghai Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. 2, 2′-Azinobis-(3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulphonate) (ABTS), potassium persulfate, and 2, 2-diphenyl-1-picrylhy-drazyl free radical (DPPH•, a nitrogen-centered free radical) were purchased from Aladdin. Lipopolysaccharide (LPS) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. DSS (Molecular weight 36–50 kDa) was purchased from MP Biomedicals, Inc. (Irvine, CA, United States). CCK-8 was purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). Mouse superoxide dismutase (SOD), malondialdehyde (MDA), and glutathione (GSH) assay kits were purchased from Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute. Propidium iodide/Annexin V-FITC was purchased from Novizan Biotechnology Co., Ltd. All aqueous solutions were prepared with deionized water (18.2 MΩ cm, Millipore). The primer sequences for RT-qPCR in mice are presented in Table S1.

CP-CDs were synthesized by direct placement of polyethylene polyamine and citric acid using a hydrothermal method. The citric acid (3.8 g) and polyethylene polyamine (0.8 mL) were first dissolved in 60 mL of double distilled water (ddH2O), then the solution was transferred to a teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 ℃ for 24 h. After the supernatant containing CP-CDs of the reaction solution was pretreated by 0.22 μm membrane filtration and dialyzed in a dialysis bag (500 kDa) dialysis for 2 days for further purification. The purified carbon dots solution was placed in a freeze dryer for freeze-drying for 48 h to obtain CP-CDs.

The characterization parameters of CP-CDs (morphology, sizes, etc.) were obtained by transmission electron microscope (JEM-F200). The crystal structure of the CP-CDs was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Netherlands). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were collected utilizing a Bruker Hyperion 2000 (Vertex80 + Hyperion2000, Germany) spectrometer to identify functional groups. The surface chemical state of CP-CDs was monitored through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). UV–visible absorption spectra of CP-CDs were measured utilizing a UV-6100 spectrophotometer. Apoptosis was measured by BD flow cytometry (BD FACSCalibur). ESR measurement was conducted on an X-band benchtop electron paramagnetic resonance spectrometer (EMXnano, China). ROS fluorescence images were acquired by laser confocal microscopy (OLYMPUS FV1000 + IX81).

DPPH free radical (DPPH•) scavenging activity: Aqueous solutions of CP-CDs at different concentrations were mixed with 1 mL of 0.05 mM DPPH• ethanol solution (DPPH• is long-lived nitrogen containing dark purple free radical that turn yellow once they interact with antioxidants) to give final concentrations of 0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 140, 160, 180, and 200 μg/mL of CP-CDs. After incubation in the dark for 20 mins, the absorbance values (Ai) of each group of solutions were measured at 517 nm. The DPPH• scavenging activity of CP-CDs was determined by Eq. (1):
(1)

where Aj is the absorbance value of the DPPH• system replaced by anhydrous ethanol, and Ac is the absorbance value of the CP-CDs solution system replaced by ddH2O.

ABTS free radical (ABTS+) scavenging activity: ABTS stock solution (14 mM) was reacted with potassium persulfate (K2S2O8) solution (4.9 mM) for 12 h in the dark to produce dark green ABTS+, which turned yellow once they interacted with antioxidants of ABTS+. Subsequently, the ABTS+ solution was diluted with ddH2O until the absorbance at 734 nm was approximately 1.0. CP-CDs solution was added to 1 mL of ABTS+ solution to obtain final concentrations of 0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 140, 160, 180, and 200 μg/mL CP-CDs solution, respectively. The solution was incubated for 6 mins in the dark at 37℃, and the absorbance at 734 nm was measured using an enzyme marker (Ai). The scavenging activity of the ABTS+ was calculated from Eq. (2):
(2)

where Aj is the absorbance value of the ABTS+ system replaced by ddH2O, and Ac is the absorbance value of ABTS+ at 734 nm in the absence of CP-CDs, respectively.

We also measured the DPPH• and ABTS+ scavenging effects of CP-CDs at room temperature using electron spin resonance spectroscopy (ESR). Mix ABTS+ with an absorbance of 1.0 with 200 μg/mL CP-CDs, incubate for 6 mins, take 50 μL sample solutions in a capillary tube (inside diameter: 0.9 mm) for detection, and collect ESR spectra. Similarly, mix 0.05 mM DPPH• with 200 μg/mL CP-CDs and collect ESR spectrum after 20 mins of incubation.

Mouse monocyte-macrophage RAW264.7 and human colon epithelial cell NCM460 were purchased from ATCC. All cell lines were cultured in a medium containing 90 % DMEM basal medium and 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) in a humidified environment with 95 % air and 5 % CO2 at 37℃ [29].

Wild-type N2 C. elegans were obtained from the School of Life Sciences, University of Science and Technology of China. E. coli OP50 strain (E. coli OP50) was kept in our laboratory, OP50 was used as a food source to sustain the worms [30], but heat-killed E. coli OP50 was used during incubation. The nematodes were cultured in nematode growth medium (NGM) containing E. coli OP50 in an incubator at 20 ℃.

The nematode synchronization was to select NGM plates containing a large number of nematode eggs, rinse the plates with sterile M9 buffer, collect the rinsing solution, and discard the supernatant. Then added 3 mL of prepared nematode lysate (nematode lysate (19.5 mL system): add 1 mL NaOH (5 M), 2.5 mL of NaClO (0.169 M), and 16 mL of ddH2O to the beaker, stir well, and place the mixture in a high-pressure autoclave at 121℃ for 30 mins to sterilize.) and 7 mL of sterile M9 buffer, centrifuge at 1000 rpm for 5 mins at 4 ℃, and discarded the supernatant. The precipitate was resuspended with M9 buffer and centrifuged, and this was repeated three times until the hypochlorite had no discernible odor. The precipitate was re-suspended with M9 buffer, gently dripped on NGM containing OP50, and incubated in a thermostatic incubator. After 24 h the eggs hatch, and about 48 h later, the nematode enters L4 stage C. elegans, the nematode synchronization is completed, and the L4 stage nematodes is placed for use.

The human colon epithelial cells (NCM460) were cultivated in a cell incubator for for 12 h after being added at a density of 8000 cells per well of the 96-well plate. Then, CP-CDs was diluted with fresh medium to a final concentration of 0–400 μg/mL, and 100 μL was added to each well of the 96-well plate to replace the original medium, and the co-culture was continued for 24 h [31]. The cell survival rates of the different treatment groups were determined using the CCK-8 method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antioxidant of the CP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The experiment was divided into the control group, H2O2 group, and H2O2 + CP-CDs group, and NCM460 cells were inoculated into 6-well plates (105 cells/well) according to the groups. Except for the control group, the cells were stimulated by the addition of 1 mM H2O2, while the H2O2 + CP-CDs group was added with final concentrations of 20, 100, and 200 μg/mL CD-CDs solution, respectively. After 12 h of co-culture, the cells were labeled with Annexin V-FITC/PI double staining apoptosis detection kit. The apoptosis rate of cells in different treatment groups was detected by flow cytometry and quantified by FlowJo 10.8.1 software. The cell survival rates of the different groups were determined using the CCK-8 method.

Inoculate RAW264.7 cells at a density of 105 cells per well into a laser confocal dish and culture for 6 h. The medium containing lipopolysaccharide (LPS) (1 μg/mL) was replaced by the original medium for overnight incubation. Then the treatment group was added to CP-CDs (20, 100, 200 μg/mL) and continued incubation for 24 h. Afterward, each group was added 1 mL 2′, 7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) dyed ROS and incubated at 37℃ for 20 mins. After washing twice with phosphate buffer saline (PBS), the cells were imaged using confocal laser microscopy, and ROS levels were quantified with Image j mean fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro anti-inflammation of the CP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Mouse monocyte-macrophage RAW264.7 cells were inoculated into 6-well plates (105 cells/well), which were divided into control, lipopolysaccharide (LPS), and LPS + CP-CDs groups. Firstly, RAW264.7 cells were induced into M1 macrophages with LPS (final concentration of 1 μg/mL), and the control group was cultured normally. After 12 h, CP-CDs with final concentrations of 20, 100, and 200 μg/mL were added to the intervention group to continue the incubation for 24 h, and the same amount of double-distilled water was added to the control group and the LPS group. 24 h later, the cells of the groups were collected and the inflammation of the cells was measured by the Elisa kit to determine the expression of inflammatory cytokines. At the same time, RNA was extracted from the cells in each group, RNA concentration was calculated, and the RNA was reverse transcribed into cDNA, then the relative expression levels of the relevant inflammatory genes were determined by RT-qPCR. The 2 - ΔΔCt method was used to determine the expression of inflammatory cytokines at the RNA level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo biocompatibility of the CP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The nematodes in the negative control group were fed with E. coli OP50, while the nematodes in the experimental group were fed with OP50 mixed with CP-CDs (20, 100, 200 μg/mL) at different final concentrations. Synchronized L4-stage nematodes were transferred to corresponding NGM plate groups of at least 3 plates each, each containing approximately 200 nematodes, and randomly selected for body length and motion count assessment.

Safety assessment in mice was performed at an optimal therapeutic dose of 10 mg/kg. The CP-CDs group was given 10 mg/kg of CP-CDs for 14 consecutive days, and control group was given the same dose of water. 14 days later, the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney of each group of mice were taken for hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Therapeutic effect of the CP-CDs in colitis mice
ce:section.ce:para: C57BL/6 mice were randomly divided into the control group, dextran sodium sulfate-induced colitis model group (DSS group), and CP-CDs groups (DSS + CP-CDs). The CP-CDs groups were further divided into 1 mg/kg, 5 mg/kg, and 10 mg/kg carbon dots group. After one week of adaptive feeding, the colitis model was successfully constructed by giving 2.5 % DSS water for seven days and the control group was given the same amount of drinking water [32]. Subsequently, mice in the control group and DSS group were given 200 μL normal saline, and the treatment groups were given different doses of CP-CDs, which were stopped after 14 consecutive days. The body weights of the mice were recorded daily, the stool and stool blood of the mice were observed, and colon tissues were collected on the 28th day to measure the length of the colon, pathological sections were made, and clinical scores were performed after H&E staining. All animal experiments and procedures were performed with the approval of the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Anhui University (Approval No.: IACUC(AHU)-2023-025).

All experiments were performed at least three times and results are expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Statistical analyses were performed using GraphPad Prism 8. Statistical differences between multiple groups were assessed using two-sided Student's t-tests and univariate or two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA).

A new type of metal-free CP-CDs was synthesized by hydrothermal method using polyethylene polyamine and citric acid as precursors. The morphology and structure of CP-CDs were investigated. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 2 a) indicated that the spherical CP-CDs were successfully synthesized, and the size statistical results conform to Gaussian curves with an average particle diameter of 5.0 ± 0.89 nm (Fig. 2b). High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) analysis revealed (Fig. 2c) that the crystalline structure existed in the CP-CDs with a lattice spacing of 0.218 nm, corresponding to the (111) crystal face of graphite [33]. The XRD pattern (Fig. 2d) exhibited a diffraction peak of CP-CDs (2θ = 21.2°), which was attributed to the (002) crystal plane of graphite [34]. The protoplasmic composition and chemical state of CP-CDs were measured by FTIR and XPS (Fig. 2e, f). FTIR analysis depicted the peaks at 3421 and 2947 cm−1 may belong to the peaks of O–H and C–H, respectively [11,31]. The peaks at 1644 and 1563 cm−1 were observed, which may be assigned to the stretching C=O and N–H mode, respectively [9,13]. Additionally, the FTIR spectrum showed the presence of stretching C–N and C–O modes at 1400 and 1298 cm−1, respectively [22,35,36]. Further, three major peaks of C (71.44 At%), O (16.89 At%), and N (11.67 At%) were found in the XPS spectra (Fig. 2f). The C1s spectra (Fig. 2g) confirmed the presence of C–C (284.9 eV), C-O/C-N (286.05 eV) and C = O (288.2 eV), respectively [13,35]. The two fitted peaks at 399.9 eV and 400.6 eV in the N1s spectra represented the existence of pyrrolic-N and graphitic-N (Fig. 2h) [36,37]. The peaks at 531.5 and 532.5 eV can be ascribed to C–O and C=O, respectively [36,38] (Fig. 2i). The above results depict a clear core–shell structure of the prepared CP-CDs, and there are abundant functional groups on the surface, which can endow them with good water solubility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro anti-oxidative and anti-inflammatory effects of the CP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the free radical scavenging capacity of CP-CDs, ABTS+ and DPPH• were used as indicators to detect using UV spectroscopy. Fig. 3 a illustrated that ABTS+ was inhibited and characteristic absorption gradually decreased as the concentration of CP-CDs increased. This is intuitively manifested as the disappearance of green color when different concentrations of CP-CDs were added to the ABTS+ mixed solution (the inset in Fig. 3a). Therefore, the antioxidant capacity can be obtained by calculating by calculating the absorption inhibition rate of CP-CDs on ABTS+ at 734 nm. As shown in Fig. 3b, the clearance rate of CP-CDs to ABTS+ free radical can reach about 100 % with 200 μg/mL. Moreover, the electron spin resonance (ESR) signal intensity of ABTS+ was significantly reduced after the addition of 200 μg/mL CP-CDs, which once again proved that CP-CDs could effectively eliminate ABTS+ (Fig. S1a). Similarly, CP-CDs also proved good scavenging efficiency against DPPH• (Fig. S1b–d), demonstrating that CP-CDs possess good antioxidant properties.

As a potential nanomedicine, the low toxicity of CP-CDs was critical for their further biomedical applications. The cytotoxicity of CP-CDs was studied by NCM460 cells using CCK-8 kit assays. When the concentration of CP-CDs increased from 0 to 400 μg/mL, the cell survival rate remained more than 80 % (Fig. 3c), indicating low toxicity of CP-CDs. Further, the oxidative stress model of hydrogen peroxide in vitro was established by stimulating NCM460 cells with H2O2 to investigate the antioxidant effect of CP-CDs. As shown in Fig. 3d, the capability of CP-CDs to improve oxidative damage in NCM460 cells after introducing 1 mM exogenous H2O2 was detected through apoptosis experiments. The results evidenced that the NCM460 cells in the control group were essentially free of apoptosis, while those in the H2O2 group were divided into three subgroups, with early and late apoptotic cells accounting for 26.2 % and 13.9 %, respectively. More importantly, the apoptosis rate of cells decreased after treatment with different concentrations of CP-CDs, and the survival rate increased by 24.5 % at 200 μg/mL, indicating that CP-CDs could alleviate cell apoptosis induced by exogenous H2O2. In addition, the cell viability experiments of the NCM460 cell line under inflammatory conditions induced by 1 mM H2O2 also confirmed this conclusion. As shown in Fig. S2, the cell viability of the H2O2-treated group was only about 56 %, and that gradually improved as the concentration of CP-CDs added increased from 20 μg/mL to 400 μg/mL. The above results indicated that CP-CDs could attenuate apoptosis and oxidative stress induced by exogenous H2O2. Correspondingly, the ROS detection probe (2′, 7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate, DCFH-DA) was used to assess ROS levels during the incubation of RAW264.7 cells to verify the above results [39]. As depicted in Fig. S3a, there was no obvious fluorescence in the control group, while clear and visible green fluorescence was observed in the modeling group. As the concentration of CP-CDs increased, the results in Fig. S3a and b showed a gradual decrease in the intracellular fluorescence intensity. Altogether, these results indicate that CP-CDs were an effective antioxidant, which is promising in the treatment of oxidative damage-related diseases.

Since oxidative stress is often considered a trigger for inflammatory response, a typical in vitro inflammatory model was established using lipopolysaccharide-induced RAW264.7 cells to explore whether the antioxidant capacity of CP-CDs also exhibited anti-inflammatory effects [40]. The mRNA levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines (tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α) and interleukin-1β (IL-1β)) in the cells of Fig. 3e and f increased, indicating the successful production of the inflammation model. On the contrary, the mRNA levels of TNF-α and IL-1β were significantly downregulated with different concentrations of CP-CDs intervening. The Elisa assay to evaluate TNF-α and IL-1β (Fig. 3g and h) levels in cell supernatants also corresponded to this result. The above results indicate that CP-CDs with ROS scavenging capability can simultaneously down-regulate the levels of inflammatory cytokines, thereby alleviating cellular inflammation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Therapeutic effect of CP-CDs on oxidative damage of nematodes
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the in vitro experimental results of CP-CDs, we further selected Caenorhabditis elegans, a common model organism for studying mammalian senescence [41], to investigate their antioxidant application value in vivo. Firstly, the biocompatibility of CP-CDs in nematodes was evaluated by measuring the body length and number of exercises in Fig. 4 a–c. Feeding nematode eggs with pure E. coli OP50 and OP50 + CP-CDs (20, 100, and 200 μg/mL, respectively) for 7 continuous days to synchronize the L4-stage. It was found that the nematode body length of the control group (OP50) and the experimental group (OP50 + CP-CDs) reached about 1000 μm, and there were no significant differences between the two groups (Fig. 4a, b). After that, 10 nematodes were randomly selected and placed in the NGM dish, and the motility of the two groups of nematodes was detected based on several exercises within 30 s. As shown in Fig. 4c, although the locomotor frequency of nematodes in the experimental group slightly increased, and that of the nematodes fed with OP50 + 200 μg/mL CP-CDs increased by 7.7 % increase compared to the negative control group, there was also no significant difference, indicating that CP-CDs had almost no effect on nematode growth and locomotor frequency.

The oxidative stress model was established after the intervention of 0.5 mM juglone in the synchronized nematodes to further validate the antioxidant capacity of CP-CDs [41,42], and the ROS content of each group was monitored by the ROS detection probe. As shown in Fig. 4d, e, the juglone group had the highest ROS content, and the ROS content in the experimental group (juglone + CP-CDs) nematodes gradually decreased with increasing concentrations of CP-CDs. The nematodes in the juglone + 200 μg/mL CP-CDs group exhibited ROS levels comparable to those in the control group. Next, the effects of CP-CDs on superoxide dismutase (SOD), glutathione (GSH), total antioxidant capacity (T-AOC), and malondialdehyde (MDA) levels in nematode were evaluated. As shown in Fig. 4f, the level of intracellular SOD decreased significantly after the introduction of juglone and gradually increased after the addition of CP-CDs, which showed a dose-dependent relationship with CP-CDs concentration. The content of GSH and T-AOC in nematodes can serve as another important factor to measure the level of oxidative stress in nematodes [43]. The content of GSH and T-AOC in nematodes reduced to about 50 % and 30 % respectively under the stimulation of juglone, and then gradually increased after adding CP-CDs (Fig. 4g, h). Moreover, CP-CDs can efficiently inhibit juglone-induced elevation of MDA activity (Fig. 4i). Correspondingly, the results in the mRNA level showed that intervention of CP-CDs up-regulated the mRNA levels of oxidative stress-related genes SOD-5, GST-10, and CTL-1 in C. elegans (Fig. 4j–l). The transcription factor Skn-1 in nematodes plays an important role in enhancing oxidative stress resistance by up-regulating the transcript levels of genes involved in detoxification, scavenging of free radical, and other oxidatively active molecules [44,45]. Fig. 4m illustrated that CP-CDs also significantly upregulated the expression of Skn-1 mRNA. The above results proved that the intervention of CP-CDs improved the nematode's anti-oxidative stress capacity, reduced oxidative damage, and had good antioxidant effects, providing a basis for reducing inflammation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Therapeutic effect of CP-CDs on DSS-induced colitis in mice
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the favorable biocompatibility and antioxidant properties of CP-CDs, the DSS-induced mice model was applied to further investigate the anti-inflammatory application of CP-CDs. Randomly selected male C57BL/6 mice (mean weight 20 ± 2 g; age 7 weeks) were kept in a 12 h light cycle, fed, and watered for 1 week to acclimatize to the environment. Afterward, they were randomly divided into five groups (n = 6): control group, dextran sodium sulfate-induced colitis model group (DSS group), and CP-CDs intervention group (low concentration CP-CDs intervention group (1 mg/kg), medium concentration CP-CDs intervention group (5 mg/kg), and high concentration CP-CDs intervention carbon dots group (10 mg/kg)). The colitis model was successfully constructed by giving 2.5 % DSS water for seven days [46]. Subsequently, as shown in Fig. 5 a, everyday gavaged of 200 μL normal saline and different doses of CP-CDs for 14 continuous days in the DSS group and CP-CDs group mice, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5b, the mice showed significant weight loss after 7 days of the DSS modeling, and this trend of weight loss in colitis mice was alleviated with 14 continuous days of CP-CDs intervention. The DAI scores of mice weight in DSS and CP-CDs groups reached about 8 on the 7th day of modeling. Compared with the DAI score of DSS group, which fluctuated around 8 for 14 days, the CP-CDs group exhibited a downward trend after the administration of CP-CDs (Fig. S4). Meanwhile, the colon length statistics of mice showed that CP-CDs alleviated colon shortening in mice with colitis, suggesting the good therapeutic effect of CP-CDs (Fig. 5c, 5d, and Fig. S5).

At the onset of IBD, the large amount of inflammatory cytokines and inflammatory mediators produced by the intestinal mucosa can damage the intestinal epithelial cells, which induces apoptosis of the epithelial cells [47]. As shown in Fig. 5e, f, immunohistochemical results showed that the expression of ZO-1, a key protein of TJ, was reduced in the colon tissue of the DSS group and significantly increased after CP-CDs intervention compared with the control group and showed a dose-dependent relationship with the concentration of CP-CDs. Alcian blue stain showed that the mucin area and number of cup cells in colitis mice significantly increased after treatment with different concentrations of CP-CDs, compared to the DSS group (Fig. S6a and b). Similarly, the H&E staining also demonstrated that CP-CDs were able to restore mice intestinal epithelial tissue (Fig. S6c). Finally, analysis of mRNA expression of tight junction proteins, including Occludin (Fig. 5g), Claudin-1 (Fig. 5h), ZO-1 (Fig. 5i), and mucin 2 (MUC-2) (Fig. 5j) in mice colon further confirmed that CP-CDs could attenuate pathological damage to the intestinal epithelial barrier by up-regulating the expression of TJ-associated proteins.

CP-CDs were known to show good biocompatibility in cells and nematodes, and then the toxicity of CP-CDs in mice was systematically evaluated to ensure their safety. The safety assessment was performed at an optimal therapeutic dose of 10 mg/kg. After 14 consecutive days of CP-CDs administration in mice, hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining of major organs [28,48], including heart, liver, spleen, lungs, kidneys, no structural and pathological changes were observed, indicating good biocompatibility of CP-CDs in mice at therapeutic doses (Fig. S7). Therefore, the above data suggested that CP-CDs intervention restored mice's body weight and colon length protected the integrity of the intestinal barrier, and was a potential therapeutic drug for colitis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CP-CDs regulate oxidative stress and down-regulates NLRP3 pathway inflammatory cytokines in colitis mice
ce:section.ce:para: Further exploration of the potential mechanism of CP-CDs in alleviating DSS-induced acute colitis revealed that they can regulate oxidative stress levels and effectively down-regulate inflammatory cytokines in the colon (Fig. 6 ). On the one hand, measurements of colonic tissue oxidative stress indexes in each group further confirmed that CP-CDs can significantly increase the levels of GSH and SOD, and effectively inhibit the increase of MDA activity induced by DSS (Fig. 6a–c). Both GSH and SOD play an important role in the antioxidant process of the body [11]. MDA is a peroxidation marker in the colonic tissue of mice with acute lipid colitis [49]. These substances in the colon change as the concentration of CP-CDs increases, reflecting the role of CP-CDs in regulating oxidative stress. On the other hand, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay proved that CP-CDs treatment could decrease the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-α and IL-1β (Fig. 6d and e) and increase the expression of anti-inflammatory cytokines interleukin-10 (IL-10) (Fig. 6f).

The inflammasome is a protein complex of the innate immune system that is activated by exogenous pathogens or endogenous danger signals to initiate an inflammatory response [50]. Studies have found that ROS is the main mediator of NLRP3 inflammasome activation and ROS inhibitors can block the activation of NLRP3 inflammasomes, reduce cellular inflammatory damage, and delay disease progression [51,52]. Based on the antioxidant ability of CP-CDs, we further investigated their impact on the inflammasome signaling pathway and found that intervention with CP-CDs could significantly reduce the mRNA expression of NLRP3, ASC, Caspase-1, and IL-1β in the pathway (Fig. 6g–j). Therefore, it was speculated that CP-CDs can effectively inhibit the activation of NLRP3 typical inflammasome to alleviate intestinal inflammation (Fig. 6k) [53]. These results indicate that the intervention of CP-CDs can regulate the level of oxidative stress, affect the status of inflammasome pathways, reduce inflammatory cytokines, and thus alleviate inflammation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CP-CDs regulate the intestinal microbiota in colitis mice
ce:section.ce:para: The imbalance of gut microbiota is implicated in inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) [54]. As a result of the above colitis mouse results, 10 mg/kg CP-CDs has the best therapeutic effect. To test whether CP-CDs alleviate gut microbiota dysbiosis, changes in gut microbiota (control group, DSS group, and 10 mg/kg CP-CDs group) were investigated by sequencing the 16S rRNA gene in region V4 [55]. As shown in the Venn diagram in Fig. 7 a, the control group and DSS group contain 42 common microorganisms, the CP-CDs group, and DSS group contain 31 common microorganisms, while the control group and CP-CDs group contain 85 common microorganisms. The results showed that DSS modeling could lead to intestinal bacterial disorder, and the types of intestinal microbes in mice treated with CP-CDs were more similar to those in the control group. The effect of CP-CDs on the structure of the gut microbiota was analyzed using principal coordinate analysis (PCoA). The distance between the two groups showed that the CP-CDs group deviated from the structure of the gut microbiota of the DSS group and was more similar to the gut microbiota of the control group (Fig. 7b). In addition, β-diversity analyses based on clustered trees (Fig. S8) depicted that the gut microbes of mice are divided into two main branches: the DSS group and the control group (Control and CP-CDs groups), which were in general agreement with the results of PCoA analyses.

Further, the abundance of species and community composition at the phylum level were presented in Fig. 7c. Typically, in healthy individuals, Firmicutes and Bacteroidetes account for approximately 90 % of the gut microbiotas [56]. Firmicutes are considered beneficial bacteria that produce metabolites to maintain the intestinal barrier and mucosal immunity, thus playing an important role in intestinal homeostasis [57]. Moreover, Proteobacteria were considered to be the major pathogen due to the production of endotoxins [58]. Fusobacterium was associated with colorectal cancer (CRC) tissues, and Fusobacterium nucleatum can serve as a biomarker for screening high-risk individuals for CRC [59]. Compared with the DSS group, the CP-CDs group had an increase in the abundance of Firmicutes, and a decrease in Proteobacteria and Fusobacteriota, approaching the level of the control group, suggesting that CP-CDs may had a positive effect on colitis by mediating changes in gut microbiota abundance.

At the genus level, the more abundant genera in the sample were unclassified_Muribaculacea, Ligilactobacillus, Dubosiella, and Lactobacillus (Fig. 7d). Unclassified_Muribaculacea has been reported to reduce inflammation, inhibit harmful bacteria and oxidative stress, and ameliorate intestinal mucosal inflammation [60]. Lactobacillus can inhibit the expression of TNF-α, improve antioxidant capacity, promote the increase of protocells and the maintenance of intestinal barrier function, and directly compete with pathogenic bacteria, thereby helping to improve weight loss [13,61]. The analysis results in Fig. 7d presented that although unclassified_Muribaculacea and Lactobacillus decreased, and Bacteroides increased in the DSS group compared to the control group, this trend took a favorable turn after CP-CDs treatment. Subsequently, we further conducted statistical differences analysis on the relative abundance changes of several typical intestinal bacteria. Fig. e-g showed that the relative abundance of Ligilactobacillus and Enterorhabduus significantly increased after CP-CDs treatment, compared with the DSS group, while the relative abundance of harmful bacteria unclassified_Clostridia_UCG_014 decreased. Accordingly, Ligilactobacillus is a type of Lactobacillus that often exhibits probiotic properties, such as antimicrobial activity, immune effects, and the ability to regulate the gut microbiota [62]. Enterorhabdus was negatively correlated with pro-inflammatory cytokines, which play a positive role in intestinal homeostasis [63,64]. And the decreased abundance of unclassified_Clostridia_UCG_014 can cause gut microbial remodeling [65]. In addition, the relative abundance of harmful bacteria (Parastutterella and Turicibacter), in colitis, also decreased in the CP-CDs group (Fig. S9) [66]. Therefore, the above results suggested that another important mechanism by which CP-CDs alleviate colitis may result from their regulation of the gut microbiota.

In summary, a novel metal-free CP-CDs with good free radical scavenging ability and biocompatibility was synthesized through hydrothermal citric acid and polyethylene polyamine. At the cellular level, CP-CDs exhibited low toxicity, and significant antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects. In vivo, nematode experiments not only demonstrated the biosafety of CP-CDs but also further demonstrated that CP-CDs can alleviate oxidative stress damage induced by juglone. Further applying CP-CDs to the mice model of DSS-induced colitis, the weight, colon length, and staining results proved that CP-CDs had a relieving effect on colitis. Exploring the therapeutic mechanism of CP-CDs, it was found that they could alleviate DSS-induced colitis by regulating oxidative stress, down-regulating NLRP3 pathway inflammatory cytokines, and improving microbial community. In particular, CP-CDs increased the abundance of beneficial bacteria such as unclassified_Muribaculacea, Ligilactobacillus, Lactobacillus, etc., and decreased the abundance of harmful bacteria such as unclassified_Clostridia_UCG_014 and Turicibacter. This finding provides more possibilities for the multifactorial combination therapy of IBD and also demonstrates the enormous biological prospects of nanomedicine.
